principles.label: Love is the discovery of reality
principles.summary: Love requires the extremely difficult realization that something other than oneself is real. It involves observing and comprehending the beloved through a just and loving gaze, rather than trying to transform them or assimilate them into an egotistical fantasy.
principles.details: Love alerts us to the independent existence of others, allowing us to perceive their unutterable particularity. It is the foundation of both good art and morality.
principles.representative_quote: Love is the extremely difficult realisation that something other than oneself is real. Love and so art and morals, is the discovery of reality.

principles.label: Morality is an everyday practice of continuous attention
principles.summary: Virtue is woven into the fabric of daily life through small, cumulative efforts of imagination and attention, rather than being a series of decisive, heroic leaps of the will.
principles.details: The moral life is ongoing. We build up structures of value around us through continuous attention, so that at crucial moments of choice, most of the business of choosing is already over.
principles.representative_quote: The task of attention goes on all the time, and at apparently empty and everyday moments we are looking, making those little peering efforts of imagination which have such important cumulative results.

principles.label: Keep philosophy and literature strictly separate
principles.summary: Philosophy and literature have radically different aims and styles. Philosophy aims to clarify and is akin to science, while literature often mystifies, entertains, and relies on allusion and ambiguity.
principles.details: While both are fundamentally truth-seeking activities, injecting overt philosophical theories into a novel damages the art. Philosophy should only appear in fiction in a background, roundabout sense related to moral problems.
principles.representative_quote: These two branches of thought have such different aims and such different styles. And I feel very strongly that one should keep them apart from each other.

principles.label: Art and morals share the same essence
principles.summary: Goodness and beauty are part of the same structure. Both require us to overcome our selfish egos and pay loving attention to the reality of other individuals.
principles.details: Aesthetic situations are actual cases of morals, requiring the same virtues (bravery, truthfulness, patience, humility) as ethical behavior.
principles.representative_quote: Art and morals are, with certain provisos… one. Their essence is the same. The essence of both of them is love.

principles.label: Morality is objective and tied to the world
principles.summary: Morality is not subjective or a mere matter of taste. Good is a recognizable fact, and value concepts are inextricably tied to the world.
principles.details: Separating fact from value wrongly reduces value to mere emotive language. To distinguish good from bad, we must see the world as it really is, unclouded by personal desires.
principles.representative_quote: To tell good from bad, though, we would need to see the world as it really is, not as we want to see it...

principles.label: The novel is an inherently tragicomic form
principles.summary: The traditional novel reflects ordinary human life, which is both terrible and profoundly funny. Pure tragedy belongs to the theater, while the novel relies on humor as an essential aspect of human freedom.
principles.details: Funniness should be everywhere in the texture of a novel, reflecting the reality that even in terrible situations, human life is full of jokes.
principles.representative_quote: pure tragedy I think belongs to the theater the the novel is essentially tragic comic

principles.label: Great art requires the self-discipline to expel personal fantasy
principles.summary: Fantasy is a private self-consolation that obscures reality. The artist must remove this veil to respect and clearly see what is genuinely other than themselves.
principles.details: A bad novel often features a hero (a stand-in for the writer) who triumphs over all enemies, which is an exercise in pure fantasy that damages the work.
principles.representative_quote: I think art is very much concerned with removing a veil of fantasy, which normally perhaps wraps one's head as one gazes at the world.

principles.label: True moral freedom resembles obedience to reality
principles.summary: When we look patiently and lovingly at a situation, the right course of action becomes a clear necessity that we submit to, rather than an arbitrary choice of an unimpeded will.
principles.details: Freedom is not the random movement of an empty will, but the result of clear vision achieved through moral imagination and effort.
principles.representative_quote: The idea of a patient, loving, regard, directed upon a person, a thing, a situation, presents the will, not as an unimpeded movement but as something very much more like 'obedience'.

principles.label: Prolific creation is preferable to sterile perfectionism
principles.summary: Writing less does not necessarily improve the work; it just results in less art. Refusing to publish out of a desire for 'perfection' is often a cowardly way to feel superior to those who try and fail.
principles.details: Murdoch defended her high output against critics, arguing that artists understand their own weaknesses far better than critics do, and must simply continue to create.
principles.representative_quote: I do not believe that I would improve if I wrote less. The only result of that would be that there would be less of whatever there is and less of me...

principles.label: Becoming good is a matter of purifying desires
principles.summary: Desire is a fundamental source of human energy. Rather than eliminating desire, moral progress involves cultivating good desires directed toward good ends.
principles.details: Good and bad exist on a continuum in human beings, and moral improvement is the process of shifting one's desires toward the good.
principles.representative_quote: I would think that becoming good... is a matter of purifying one's desires, having good desires, desiring something which is good.

principles.label: Writing objectifies psychological conflicts
principles.summary: Writing is a tool to externalize and examine the complex, confusing conflicts experienced within oneself and observed in the characters of others.
principles.details: The novel serves as a medium to explore human suffering, power, treachery, and bliss, often using chaotic romantic entanglements as a microcosm for human nature.
principles.representative_quote: I want to write a long long & exceedingly obscure novel objectifying the queer conflicts I find within myself & observe in the characters of others.

frameworks.label: Detailed Pre-Invention of the Novel
frameworks.summary: A method for structuring fiction by entirely inventing the plot and characters before writing the prose.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Spend a great deal of time inventing the story. 2. Detail the invention of the plot. 3. Detail the invention of the characters. 4. Visualize the entire book before starting to write the first sentence.
frameworks.representative_quote: I attach great importance to the detailed invention of the plot and the detail advention of the character so that I can see the whole thing before I start to write the first sentence

frameworks.label: Refocusing Attention
frameworks.summary: A technique for overcoming strong negative emotions or unwanted love when pure willpower fails.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Recognize that pure willpower cannot simply stop a strong emotion. 2. Acquire new objects of attention. 3. Allow this refocusing to provide energy of a different kind from a different source.
frameworks.representative_quote: Deliberately falling out of love is not a jump of the will, it is the acquiring of new objects of attention and thus of new energies as a result of refocusing

frameworks.label: Joint Attention for Conceptual Understanding
frameworks.summary: A method for understanding the aesthetic or ethical framework of another person or culture through shared focus.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Group around a common object of attention (e.g., a work of art). 2. Listen to normative-descriptive talk from an informed other in the presence of the object. 3. Share their context to gradually grasp the beliefs, concerns, and values that structure their concepts.
frameworks.representative_quote: Uses of words by persons grouped round a common object is a central and vital human activity. The art critic can help us if we are in the presence of the same object and if we know something about his scheme of concepts.

mental_models.label: The Fat Relentless Ego (The Veil of Fantasy)
mental_models.summary: The mind's tendency to fabricate an anxious, preoccupied, and falsifying veil that conceals true reality. The ego acts as a distorting filter that assimilates the 'other' into a personal fantasy world.
mental_models.details: This is the primary enemy of both art and morality. Moral progress requires piercing this veil through objectivity and unselfishness to see the world as it truly is.
mental_models.representative_quote: the enemy is the fat relentless ego

mental_models.label: Unselfing
mental_models.summary: The discipline of detaching from the ego's preoccupations to allow reality to appear. It is both an epistemic and moral virtue that clears the field for true attention.
mental_models.details: Often occasioned by great art or nature (like watching a hovering kestrel), where the ego disappears, leaving only the object of attention.
mental_models.representative_quote: I would think that people have what I would call selfless experience when – well for instance when they are looking at a great work of art.

mental_models.label: The Patient Eye of Love
mental_models.summary: Viewing moral life not as a series of discrete actions or choices, but as a continuous, just, and loving gaze directed upon individual reality.
mental_models.details: It involves observing other people with an undramatic, un-self-centered agnosticism that respects their autonomy and particularity.
mental_models.representative_quote: a just and loving gaze directed upon an individual reality

mental_models.label: Imagination vs. Fantasy
mental_models.summary: A lens for evaluating art and thought. Imagination is a creative, truth-seeking power that respects reality, while fantasy is a private, self-consoling illusion.
mental_models.details: Fantasy acts as a menace to creative imagination, resulting in bad art that fails to truthfully reflect the world.
mental_models.representative_quote: I think art is very much concerned with removing a veil of fantasy, which normally perhaps wraps one's head as one gazes at the world.

mental_models.label: Monism of Value
mental_models.summary: The belief that all forms of value—whether aesthetic, ethical, or intellectual—stem from a single, unitary structure rather than existing in isolated domains.
mental_models.details: The virtues required to be a good artist (bravery, truthfulness, humility) are fundamentally the same as those required to be a good person.
mental_models.representative_quote: Goodness and beauty are not to be contrasted but are largely part of the same structure

mental_models.label: The Divided Self
mental_models.summary: A lens for viewing human consciousness where the self is split into parts that can judge, disapprove of, or attempt to control one another.
mental_models.details: It is a basic characteristic of human consciousness that we experience internal conflict, demonstrating that one part of the self is judging another.
mental_models.representative_quote: one doesn't have to be philosopher or hold a religious view to think that one is divided, one is a divided person.

mental_models.label: The Erotic Life as a Microcosm
mental_models.summary: Using complex, chaotic romantic entanglements as a primary lens for exploring human suffering, power, treachery, and bliss.
mental_models.details: Just as other writers used war or the sea, Murdoch used the 'tragic comedy of sexual love' to reveal human nature and how love makes people act absurdly and violently.
mental_models.representative_quote: The erotic life is never still.

heuristics.label: See the whole thing first
heuristics.summary: See the whole structure of the story before you start to write the first sentence.
heuristics.representative_quote: See the whole thing before you start to write the first sentence.

heuristics.label: Trust the tale
heuristics.summary: Don't trust the writer, trust the tale.
heuristics.representative_quote: Don't trust the writer, trust the tale.

heuristics.label: Act lovingly over rationally
heuristics.summary: 'Act lovingly' translates to 'Act perfectly' better than 'Act rationally' does.
heuristics.representative_quote: 'Act lovingly' translates to 'Act perfectly' better than 'Act rationally' does.

heuristics.label: Understanding is endless
heuristics.summary: To understand other people is a task which does not come to an end.
heuristics.representative_quote: To understand other people is a task which does not come to an end.

heuristics.label: Virtue is the same in art and life
heuristics.summary: Virtue is au fond the same in the artist as in the good man.
heuristics.representative_quote: Virtue is au fond the same in the artist as in the good man.

heuristics.label: Reply to all letters
heuristics.summary: Dutifully reply to every letter received, unless the sender is mad or spiteful.
heuristics.representative_quote: Dutifully reply to every letter received, unless the sender is "mad or spiteful."

signature_quotes.label: Love is the discovery of reality
signature_quotes.summary: Defining the essence of love and its connection to discovering reality.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Love is the extremely difficult realisation that something other than oneself is real.

signature_quotes.label: The definition of attention
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the core of her moral philosophy in 'The Sovereignty of Good'.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I have used the word 'attention', which I borrow from Simone Weil, to express the idea of a just and loving gaze directed upon an individual reality.

signature_quotes.label: Art and morals are one
signature_quotes.summary: Connecting the disciplines of art and morality through the concept of love.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Art and morals are, with certain provisos… one. Their essence is the same. The essence of both of them is love.

signature_quotes.label: The enemy is the ego
signature_quotes.summary: Identifying the primary obstacle to becoming morally better.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: the enemy is the fat relentless ego

signature_quotes.label: Philosophy vs. Literature
signature_quotes.summary: Contrasting the fundamental aims of philosophical and literary writing.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Philosophy aims to clarify... And literature is very often mystification.

signature_quotes.label: Art speaks truth
signature_quotes.summary: Reflecting her philosophical view on the purpose of art.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: All art deals with the absurd and aims at the simple. Good art speaks truth. Indeed, is truth. Perhaps the only truth.

signature_quotes.label: Funniness as freedom
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the comic nature of the traditional novel and human life.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Funniness is also, I think, an aspect of freedom, something that totalitarian atmospheres can destroy.

signature_quotes.label: Choosing within the visible world
signature_quotes.summary: Arguing against the existentialist idea of the empty choosing will.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I can only choose within the world I can see in the moral sense of 'see' which implies that clear vision is a result of moral imagination and moral effort.

signature_quotes.label: The continuous task of attention
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining how morality is woven into daily life.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The task of attention goes on all the time, and at apparently empty and everyday moments we are looking, making those little peering efforts of imagination which have such important cumulative results.

anti_patterns.label: Putting philosophical theories directly into novels
anti_patterns.summary: Injecting overt philosophy into fiction damages the art.
anti_patterns.details: It ceases to be philosophy and damages the art. The harder a writer works to present their ideas, the less good the work of art is likely to become.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: As soon as philosophy gets into a novel, it ceases to be philosophy. It becomes something else.

anti_patterns.label: The Existentialist 'Empty Choosing Will'
anti_patterns.summary: Treating morality as a series of explicit, heroic choices made by an empty will.
anti_patterns.details: It ignores the continuous prior work of attention and moral vision, falsely identifying freedom only with the outward movement of choice at the last moment. True virtue is woven into the fabric of dailiness.

anti_patterns.label: Relying on 'pure will' to change strong emotions
anti_patterns.summary: Attempting to stop feelings like resentment or unwanted love through sheer willpower.
anti_patterns.details: Willpower alone cannot stop strong feelings; one must instead reorient attention to new objects to acquire new energies.

anti_patterns.label: Adopting post-structuralism and linguistic determinism
anti_patterns.summary: Believing that language speaks the individual and that all genres are fragmented.
anti_patterns.details: It strips away individual meaning and truth, reducing a person to a mere 'rendez-vous of codes', and frightens young writers away from traditional storytelling.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Your individual meaning is taken away, you are a rendez-vous of codes.

anti_patterns.label: Imposing 'form' on life and stereotyping others
anti_patterns.summary: Treating love as an exercise in judgment, instruction, and trying to quickly sum up a character.
anti_patterns.details: It leads to stereotyping and fails to respect the autonomy and unutterable particularity of the individual.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Form is the temptation of love and its peril… to round off a situation, to sum up a character.

anti_patterns.label: Using perfectionism as an excuse not to create
anti_patterns.summary: Refusing to publish or finish work out of a desire for an unrealized idea of perfection.
anti_patterns.details: It allows a person to feel superior to those who try and fail, but ultimately results in finishing nothing and nourishing a continual grudge against the world.

anti_patterns.label: The Fact/Value Dichotomy
anti_patterns.summary: Divorcing value claims from factual propositions.
anti_patterns.details: It relies on a false separation between reason and will, relegating value to mere emotive language rather than recognizing that seeing facts clearly (reality) is inherently tied to recognizing value (the Good).
